packs.name: Docker
packs.description: Protects against destructive Docker operations like system prune, volume prune, and force removal

structural_patterns.name: system-prune
structural_patterns.reason: docker system prune removes ALL unused containers, networks, images. Use 'docker system df' to preview.
structural_patterns.explanation:
docker system prune is Docker's most aggressive cleanup command. It removes:

- All stopped containers
- All networks not used by at least one container
- All dangling images (untagged)
- All dangling build cache

With -a flag, it also removes all unused images, not just dangling ones.
With --volumes flag, it removes all unused volumes (data loss!).

Preview what would be removed:
  docker system df          # Show disk usage
  docker system df -v       # Verbose with details

Safer alternative:
  docker container prune    # Only stopped containers
  docker image prune        # Only dangling images

suggestions.description: Preview what would be removed without deleting anything
Only removes items older than 24 hours
Remove only stopped containers (preserves images and volumes)
Remove only dangling images (preserves containers and volumes)

structural_patterns.name: volume-prune
structural_patterns.reason: docker volume prune removes ALL unused volumes and their data permanently.
structural_patterns.explanation:
docker volume prune permanently deletes ALL volumes not currently attached to a running container. This is extremely dangerous because:

- Database data stored in volumes is lost forever
- Application state and uploads are destroyed
- There is NO recovery mechanism

Even stopped containers' volumes are considered 'unused' and will be deleted.

Preview before pruning:
  docker volume ls                    # List all volumes
  docker volume ls -f dangling=true   # Show only unused

Safer approach:
  docker volume rm <specific-volume>  # Remove by name

suggestions.description: List unused volumes first to review what would be deleted
Remove specific volumes by name instead of all unused
Inspect volume contents and metadata before removal

structural_patterns.name: network-prune
structural_patterns.reason: docker network prune removes ALL unused networks.
structural_patterns.explanation:
docker network prune removes all user-defined networks not used by any container. While less destructive than volume prune, it can still cause issues:

- Custom network configurations are lost
- Containers may fail to communicate after restart
- Service discovery between containers breaks

Preview unused networks:
  docker network ls
  docker network ls -f dangling=true

Safer alternative:
  docker network rm <specific-network>

suggestions.description: List all networks to review before pruning
Remove specific networks by name instead of all unused

structural_patterns.name: image-prune
structural_patterns.reason: docker image prune removes unused images. Use 'docker images' to review first.
structural_patterns.explanation:
docker image prune removes 'dangling' images (untagged layers). With -a flag, it removes ALL images not used by existing containers.

Consequences:
- Build cache layers are deleted (slower rebuilds)
- With -a: base images must be re-pulled

Preview what would be removed:
  docker images -f dangling=true
  docker images                       # With -a flag

Usually safe, but may slow down builds.

suggestions.description: List dangling images first to see what would be removed
Remove specific images by ID or tag

structural_patterns.name: container-prune
structural_patterns.reason: docker container prune removes ALL stopped containers.
structural_patterns.explanation:
docker container prune removes all stopped containers. This is relatively safe but can cause issues:

- Container logs are lost
- Container filesystem layers are deleted
- Cannot restart or inspect removed containers

Preview stopped containers:
  docker ps -a -f status=exited
  docker ps -a -f status=created

Consider keeping recent containers for debugging.

suggestions.description: List stopped containers first to review before removal
Remove specific containers instead of all stopped

structural_patterns.name: rm-force
structural_patterns.reason: docker rm -f forcibly removes containers, potentially losing data.
structural_patterns.explanation:
docker rm -f forcibly stops and removes containers. This is dangerous because:

- Running processes are killed immediately (SIGKILL)
- No graceful shutdown - data may be corrupted
- In-flight requests are dropped
- Uncommitted data in the container is lost

Safer approach:
  docker stop <container>  # Graceful shutdown (SIGTERM)
  docker rm <container>    # Then remove

Check container status first:
  docker ps -a | grep <container>

suggestions.description: Graceful shutdown with SIGTERM before removal
Remove stopped containers with confirmation prompt
Check container status before removal

structural_patterns.name: rmi-force
structural_patterns.reason: docker rmi -f forcibly removes images even if in use.
structural_patterns.explanation:
docker rmi -f forcibly removes images, even if containers are using them. This can cause:

- Running containers to fail on restart
- Broken references to deleted layers
- Loss of build cache

Check what's using the image:
  docker ps -a --filter ancestor=<image>

Safer approach:
  docker rmi <image>  # Fails safely if in use

suggestions.description: Remove without force - fails safely if image is in use
Remove only dangling (untagged) images
Check what containers are using the image first

structural_patterns.name: volume-rm
structural_patterns.reason: docker volume rm permanently deletes volumes and their data.
structural_patterns.explanation:
docker volume rm permanently deletes named volumes and all data stored in them. This is irreversible:

- Database files are gone
- User uploads are lost
- Configuration data is destroyed
- No trash or undo mechanism exists

Check volume contents first:
  docker run --rm -v <volume>:/data alpine ls -la /data

Consider backing up:
  docker run --rm -v <volume>:/data -v $(pwd):/backup alpine \
    tar czf /backup/volume-backup.tar.gz /data

suggestions.description: Inspect volume metadata and mount point before removal
List volume contents before deletion
Backup volume data before removal

structural_patterns.name: stop-all
structural_patterns.reason: Stopping/killing all containers can disrupt services. Be specific about which containers.
structural_patterns.explanation:
This pattern stops or kills ALL running containers on the system. This is dangerous in shared environments:

- Production services go down
- Database connections are severed
- In-flight requests fail
- Other users' containers are affected

Be specific instead:
  docker stop <container-name>     # Stop by name
  docker stop $(docker ps -q -f name=myapp)  # Filter by name

Preview what would be stopped:
  docker ps --format '{{.Names}}: {{.Status}}'

suggestions.description: Stop specific containers by name
Stop containers matching a name filter
List running containers before stopping

packs.name: Docker Compose
packs.description: Protects against destructive Docker Compose operations like 'down -v' which removes volumes

structural_patterns.name: down-volumes
structural_patterns.reason: docker-compose down -v removes volumes and their data permanently.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The -v/--volumes flag causes docker-compose down to remove named volumes declared in the volumes section of the Compose file, as well as anonymous volumes attached to containers. This permanently destroys:

- Database data (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB volumes)
- User uploads and application state
- Any persistent configuration stored in volumes

Safer alternatives:
- docker-compose down: Stops and removes containers without touching volumes
- docker-compose stop: Stops containers, preserving everything
- docker volume ls: List volumes before removal

structural_patterns.name: down-rmi-all
structural_patterns.reason: docker-compose down --rmi all removes all images used by services.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The --rmi all flag removes all images used by services in the Compose file. This forces re-downloading or rebuilding images on next 'up':

- Base images must be pulled again (bandwidth, time)
- Custom built images need rebuilding
- Layers not in registry are lost

Safer alternatives:
- docker-compose down: Preserves images for faster restarts
- docker-compose down --rmi local: Only removes images without custom tag
- docker image ls: Review images before removal

structural_patterns.name: rm-volumes
structural_patterns.reason: docker-compose rm -v removes volumes attached to containers.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The -v flag with docker-compose rm removes anonymous volumes attached to the containers being removed. This can cause data loss if volumes contain:

- Application state or session data
- Cached data that takes time to rebuild
- Temporary but important processing results

Safer alternatives:
- docker-compose rm: Removes containers without volumes
- docker-compose stop: Stops without removing anything
- docker volume ls: Check what volumes exist

structural_patterns.name: rm-force
structural_patterns.reason: docker-compose rm -f forcibly removes containers without confirmation.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The -f/--force flag removes containers without asking for confirmation. While this doesn't directly cause data loss, it can be risky:

- Running containers are stopped abruptly (SIGKILL)
- No graceful shutdown for applications
- In-flight requests or transactions may be lost

Safer alternatives:
- docker-compose stop: Graceful shutdown first
- docker-compose rm: Asks for confirmation
- docker-compose ps: Check container status first

packs.name: Podman
packs.description: Protects against destructive Podman operations like system prune, volume prune, and force removal

structural_patterns.name: system-prune
structural_patterns.reason: podman system prune removes ALL unused containers, pods, images. Use 'podman system df' to preview.
structural_patterns.explanation:
podman system prune is an aggressive cleanup command that removes:

- All stopped containers
- All pods without running containers
- All dangling images (untagged)
- All dangling build cache

With -a flag, removes ALL unused images. With --volumes, removes unused volumes.

Safer alternatives:
- podman system df: Preview disk usage first
- podman container prune: Only remove stopped containers
- podman image prune: Only remove dangling images

structural_patterns.name: volume-prune
structural_patterns.reason: podman volume prune removes ALL unused volumes and their data permanently.
structural_patterns.explanation:
podman volume prune permanently deletes ALL volumes not currently in use by any container. This is extremely dangerous:

- Database data in volumes is lost forever
- Application state and uploads are destroyed
- Volumes from stopped containers are considered 'unused'
- No recovery mechanism exists

Safer alternatives:
- podman volume ls: List all volumes first
- podman volume inspect: Check volume contents
- podman volume rm <name>: Remove specific volumes

structural_patterns.name: pod-prune
structural_patterns.reason: podman pod prune removes ALL stopped pods.
structural_patterns.explanation:
podman pod prune removes all pods that are not currently running. Pods group containers together and pruning them:

- Removes all containers within the stopped pods
- Pod configuration and networking setup is lost
- Cannot restart or inspect removed pods

Safer alternatives:
- podman pod ps -a: List all pods first
- podman pod rm <pod>: Remove specific pods
- podman pod start <pod>: Restart instead of removing

structural_patterns.name: image-prune
structural_patterns.reason: podman image prune removes unused images. Use 'podman images' to review first.
structural_patterns.explanation:
podman image prune removes dangling images (untagged layers). With -a flag, removes ALL images not used by existing containers.

Consequences:
- Build cache layers are deleted (slower rebuilds)
- With -a: Base images must be re-pulled

Safer alternatives:
- podman images -f dangling=true: Preview what would be removed
- podman images: Review all images
- podman rmi <image>: Remove specific images

structural_patterns.name: container-prune
structural_patterns.reason: podman container prune removes ALL stopped containers.
structural_patterns.explanation:
podman container prune removes all stopped containers. Relatively safe but:

- Container logs are lost
- Container filesystem layers are deleted
- Cannot restart or inspect removed containers

Safer alternatives:
- podman ps -a: List all containers first
- podman rm <container>: Remove specific containers
- podman start <container>: Restart instead of removing

structural_patterns.name: rm-force
structural_patterns.reason: podman rm -f forcibly removes containers, potentially losing data.
structural_patterns.explanation:
podman rm -f forcibly stops and removes containers. This is dangerous because:

- Running processes are killed immediately (SIGKILL)
- No graceful shutdown - data may be corrupted
- In-flight requests are dropped
- Uncommitted data in the container is lost

Safer alternatives:
- podman stop <container>: Graceful shutdown first
- podman rm <container>: Then remove
- podman ps: Check container status first

structural_patterns.name: rmi-force
structural_patterns.reason: podman rmi -f forcibly removes images even if in use.
structural_patterns.explanation:
podman rmi -f forcibly removes images, even if containers reference them. This can cause:

- Containers to fail on restart (missing image)
- Broken references to deleted layers
- Loss of build cache

Safer alternatives:
- podman ps -a --filter ancestor=<image>: Check what uses the image
- podman rmi <image>: Fails safely if in use
- podman images: Review images before removal

structural_patterns.name: volume-rm
structural_patterns.reason: podman volume rm permanently deletes volumes and their data.
structural_patterns.explanation:
podman volume rm permanently deletes named volumes and all data stored in them. This is irreversible:

- Database files are gone forever
- User uploads are lost
- Configuration data is destroyed
- No trash or undo mechanism

Safer alternatives:
- podman volume inspect <volume>: Check volume details
- podman run --rm -v vol:/data alpine ls -la /data: View contents
- Back up before removal
